rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical reductive double cyclization of β-dicarbonyl enol phosphates to bicyclo[n.1.0]alkanes (cyclopropane-fused carbocycles)
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction of β-dicarbonyl enol phosphate substrate 1; one-electron reduction gives radical anion 3, two-electron reduction gives vinyl anion 4 (resonance form of singlet carbene 4a), both with loss of diethyl phosphate anion

key_intermediates.name: radical anion 3a/3b (from one-electron reduction of enol phosphate, loss of phosphate)
vinyl anion 4b / singlet carbene 4a (from two-electron reduction)

electron_flow: Cathode reduces β-dicarbonyl enol phosphate 1 by one electron with loss of diethyl phosphate to give vinyl radical 3a; intramolecular radical cyclization gives monocyclic radical 5, a second radical cyclization forms bicyclic radical 6, which is further reduced at the cathode to anion 8 and protonated to deliver the bicyclo[n.1.0]alkane 7. A competing pathway via two-electron reduction to singlet carbene 4a (≡ vinyl anion 4b) is excluded by retention of alkene stereochemistry in probe substrates.

probe_reagents.name: cis/trans-disubstituted alkene substrates (examples 6 and 7 of Table I)
probe_reagents.observation: Double cyclization proceeded with retention of alkene stereochemistry, excluding free singlet carbene (3a/4a) as the reactive intermediate; an intermediate that permits isomerization (e.g., free vinyl anion) is also ruled out since complete equilibration does not occur

probe_reagents.name: ethyl carbonate analogue of enol phosphate (example 5 substrate)
probe_reagents.observation: Under identical electrochemical conditions gave 43% of the same bicyclic product, showing the leaving group can be varied and supporting a reductive C–O cleavage step

notes: Authors explicitly state mechanism is complex and not rigorously defined. Favored pathway is stepwise radical cyclization from radical anion 3a → 5 → 6 → reduction to 7. Alternatives considered and disfavored: (i) singlet carbene addition via 3a or 4a (excluded by stereoretention in examples 6/7); (ii) two-step anionic Michael route via vinyl anion 4b → 8 → intramolecular Michael; (iii) direct H-atom abstraction by 6 to give 2; (iv) reduction of 5 to 8 prior to second cyclization (disfavored due to short lifetime/ring closure outcompeting bond rotation). Loss of stereoselectivity in final protonation in example 8 indicates protonation of carbanion 8 is hindered from both cyclopentyl faces. Footnote 13 notes vinyl anions like 4b do not add intramolecularly to unactivated 1,2-disubstituted alkenes, further arguing against the anionic pathway.
